event: Early human populations inhabit the savanna and Saharan regions of present-day Mali, leaving Acheulean and later Stone Age tools.
A wetter Saharan climate supports fishing, herding, and rock-art communities across the Adrar des Ifoghas and northern Mali.
Progressive desertification of the Sahara pushes pastoral and farming peoples southward toward the Niger River valley.
Djenne-Djenno, one of sub-Saharan Africa's oldest urban centers, flourishes as a trade and ironworking town on the inland Niger Delta.
The Ghana Empire (Wagadou) rises to the northwest, controlling trans-Saharan gold and salt routes that pass through the region.
Trans-Saharan trade in gold, salt, and slaves intensifies, linking Niger River towns with North African markets.
Almoravid pressure and internal strife weaken the Ghana Empire, opening the way for new Sahelian powers.
Sundiata Keita defeats Sumanguru Kante at the Battle of Kirina and founds the Mali Empire, proclaiming the Kurukan Fuga charter.
Sundiata Keita dies, leaving a consolidated empire built on agriculture and control of the gold trade.
Mansa Musa ascends the throne of the Mali Empire, ushering in an era of legendary wealth and territorial expansion.
Mansa Musa undertakes his famous pilgrimage to Mecca, distributing so much gold in Cairo that he disrupts its economy.
Under Mansa Musa, Timbuktu and Djenne grow into renowned centers of Islamic scholarship, with the Djinguereber Mosque built by architect Es Sahili.
Moroccan traveler Ibn Battuta visits the Mali Empire and records its prosperity, justice, and Muslim court life.
Tuareg forces seize Timbuktu as the Mali Empire begins a long decline amid succession disputes and provincial revolts.
Sonni Ali of the rising Songhai Empire captures Timbuktu, supplanting Mali as the dominant Sahelian power.
Askia Muhammad seizes power in Songhai and expands an empire centered on Gao, promoting Islam and learning at Timbuktu.
A Moroccan army equipped with firearms crushes Songhai at the Battle of Tondibi, shattering the last great West African empire of the region.
The Bamana (Bambara) Kingdom of Segou emerges along the Niger, becoming a major regional power based on slave-raiding and warfare.
Biton Coulibaly consolidates the Bamana Kingdom of Segou and establishes a standing army known as the ton-dyon.
Seku Amadu founds the Islamic Massina Empire (Diina) in the inland Niger Delta, imposing a strict Fulani theocracy.
El Hadj Umar Tall's Tukulor jihad conquers Segou, building a vast but unstable Islamic state across the western Sudan.
El Hadj Umar Tall dies near Bandiagara, and the Tukulor Empire fragments under his successors.
French colonial forces capture Bamako as they push into the Niger valley during the Scramble for Africa.
Samori Toure, leader of the Wassoulou Empire who long resisted French expansion, is captured, completing French conquest of the region.
The territory is incorporated as French Sudan within the federation of French West Africa.
The Sudanese Union–African Democratic Rally (US-RDA) is founded, becoming the leading nationalist party under Modibo Keita.
French Sudan becomes the autonomous Sudanese Republic within the French Community after a constitutional referendum.
The Sudanese Republic and Senegal join to form the short-lived Mali Federation.
Mali gains full independence on September 22 under President Modibo Keita after the Mali Federation collapses, and joins the United Nations.
Mali withdraws from the CFA franc zone and introduces its own Malian franc, pursuing a socialist economic program.
Lieutenant Moussa Traore overthrows Modibo Keita in a military coup, ending the socialist First Republic.
A new constitution is approved by referendum, paving the way for a one-party state under Traore's leadership.
Moussa Traore is elected president as head of the sole legal party, the Democratic Union of the Malian People (UDPM).
Mali and Burkina Faso fight the brief Agacher Strip border conflict, known as the Christmas War.
Mass pro-democracy protests are met with deadly force, then Amadou Toumani Toure overthrows Traore and leads a transition to civilian rule.
Alpha Oumar Konare wins multiparty elections to become president, inaugurating Mali's democratic Third Republic.
A peace agreement and symbolic Flame of Peace ceremony in Timbuktu ends the first Tuareg rebellion of the early 1990s.
Amadou Toumani Toure, now a civilian, wins the presidential election and is reelected in 2007.
A Tuareg-Islamist rebellion seizes northern Mali, soldiers stage a coup against President Toure, and jihadists declare an Islamic state while destroying Timbuktu shrines.
French-led Operation Serval intervenes to retake the north, and the UN deploys the MINUSMA peacekeeping mission; Ibrahim Boubacar Keita is elected president.
The Algiers Accord peace agreement is signed between the government and northern armed groups, though violence persists.
Mounting protests and a military coup oust President Ibrahim Boubacar Keita, installing a transitional government led by Assimi Goita.
Colonel Assimi Goita seizes full power in a second coup, deepening tensions with ECOWAS and France.
French and allied forces withdraw from Mali as the junta turns to Russian Wagner mercenaries amid worsening insurgency.
Mali demands the departure of the UN MINUSMA mission, which begins withdrawing after a decade of peacekeeping.
Mali joins Burkina Faso and Niger in forming the Alliance of Sahel States and announces plans to exit ECOWAS.
